title: Forcepoint SSL Inspection Bypass by Category and Destination (`websense:cg:kv`)
dataSources: Forcepoint cloud SSE (`forcepoint:one`), on-prem Web Security (`websense:cg:kv`), Email (`forcepoint:email:kv`), DLP (`forcepoint:dlp`)
app: Forcepoint Insights SIEM App (Splunkbase 8053); Forcepoint Web Security TA (`websense:cg:kv`); Email Security KV (`forcepoint:email:kv`)
spl:
index=proxy sourcetype="websense:cg:kv" earliest=-7d
| where match(_raw,"(?i)bypass|decrypt.*no|ssl.*inspect")
| stats count by category, dst_host, user
| sort -count
description: Every bypass is a potential malware tunnel; monitoring volume ensures exceptions remain rare.
value: Security teams identify blind spots in SSL/TLS inspection coverage, ensuring that expected bypass categories (banking, healthcare) are documented while unexpected bypasses (cloud storage, uncategorized sites) are investigated and remediated.
implementation: Install the Insights SIEM app, configure HEC inputs per product line, and align indexes (`proxy`, `email`, `dlp`, `sase`). Normalize usernames to enterprise SAMAccountName.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Email.All_Email
  by Email.user, Email.action, Email.src, Email.dest span=1h
| sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Forcepoint Web Security

knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Add-on for Forcepoint Web Security on the Search Head/HF. Data in `index=proxy` with `sourcetype=websense:cg:kv`.
• Forcepoint Web Security with SSL inspection enabled. The Content Gateway performs man-in-the-middle TLS decryption for inspectable traffic. Certain categories (banking, healthcare) are typically bypassed for privacy/compliance.
• Key fields: `ssl_inspected` (yes/no), `ssl_bypass_reason` (category_exempt/pinned_cert/user_exempt/error), `category`, `dest`, `url`, `user`, `bytes_out`.
• Create a `fp_ssl_bypass_policy.csv` lookup: `category,expected_bypass,reason` documenting which categories should and should not be bypassed.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify SSL inspection fields are populated:
```spl
index=proxy sourcetype="websense:cg:kv" earliest=-7d
| stats count by ssl_inspected, ssl_bypass_reason
```
You should see a mix of `ssl_inspected=yes` (decrypted traffic) and `ssl_inspected=no` (bypassed). If `ssl_inspected` is always null, the Content Gateway may not be logging SSL inspection status.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — SSL bypass by category and destination:
```spl
index=proxy sourcetype="websense:cg:kv" earliest=-7d
| where ssl_inspected="no" OR match(lower(_raw), "bypass|decrypt.*no|ssl.*skip")
| stats count as bypassed_sessions dc(user) as users dc(dest) as destinations sum(bytes_out) as upload_bytes by category, ssl_bypass_reason
| lookup fp_ssl_bypass_policy.csv category OUTPUT expected_bypass
| eval unexpected=if(expected_bypass!="yes" AND isnull(expected_bypass), "UNEXPECTED", "EXPECTED")
| eval upload_mb=round(upload_bytes/1048576, 1)
| sort - unexpected, - bypassed_sessions
```

Understanding this SPL: Identifies which traffic categories are bypassing SSL inspection and whether the bypass is expected per policy. Unexpected bypasses create blind spots — malware can hide in encrypted traffic to destinations that should be inspected. Common legitimate bypasses: banking (regulatory), healthcare portals (HIPAA), certificate-pinned applications (some financial apps). Concerning bypasses: 'Uncategorized' (unknown sites), 'Cloud Storage' (potential exfiltration), or any category with high upload volume.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In Forcepoint Security Manager: SSL > Inspection Bypass List. Compare with the categories showing bypass in Splunk.
(b) Visit a site in a bypass category and verify `ssl_inspected=no` in Splunk.
(c) Visit a site in an inspected category and verify `ssl_inspected=yes`.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Forcepoint — SSL Inspection Coverage"):
• Row 1 — Single-value: "SSL inspection rate (%)", "Bypassed categories", "Unexpected bypasses".
• Row 2 — Pie chart: inspected vs bypassed traffic.
• Row 3 — Table: bypassed categories with reason, expected/unexpected flag, upload volume.

Alert: Trigger when an unexpected category starts generating SSL bypass events.

Runbook:
1. **Unexpected bypass on cloud storage** — Security risk. Review the SSL policy and remove the exemption, or enable DLP on the bypass channel.
2. **Certificate pinning causing bypass** — The application rejects the inspection certificate. Options: add to bypass list (accept the blind spot) or configure the application to trust the inspection CA.
3. **Bypass volume suddenly increasing** — An application or CDN may have changed its certificate configuration. Investigate the top destinations.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **`ssl_inspected` always null** — SSL inspection logging may not be enabled. Check Content Gateway > SSL > Logging.
• **All traffic shows as bypassed** — The SSL inspection certificate may not be deployed, causing all connections to fail inspection.
• **Users reporting certificate errors** — The inspection CA certificate is not trusted by the client. Deploy via GPO (Windows), MDM (mobile), or manual install.
grandmaExplanation: We check which encrypted internet traffic we are able to inspect and which we skip over, making sure we are not accidentally leaving gaps where harmful content could sneak through.
